author: John Chrysostom
book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

title: Homily XXXVI on Acts xvi. 25, 26.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And at midnight Paul and Silas prayed, and sang praises unto God: and the prisoners heard them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And suddenly there was a great earthquake, so that the foundations of the prison were shaken, and immediately all the doors were opened, and every one’s bands were loosed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What could equal these souls?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These men had been scourged, had received many stripes, they had been misused, were in peril of their lives, were thrust into the inner prison, and set fast in the stocks: and for all this they did not suffer themselves to sleep, but kept vigil all the night.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark what a blessing tribulation is?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But we, in our soft beds, with none to be afraid of, pass the whole night in sleep.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But belike this is why they kept vigil, because they were in this condition.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not the tyranny of sleep could overpower them, not the smart of pain could bow them, not the fear of evil east them into helpless dejection: no, these were the very things that made them wakeful: and they were even filled with exceeding delight.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “At midnight,” it says, “and the prisoners listened to them:” it was so strange and surprising!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And suddenly there was a great earthquake, so that the foundations of the prison were shaken, and immediately, all the doors were opened, and every one’s bands were loosed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the keeper of the prison awaking out of his sleep, and seeing the prison doors open, drew out his sword, and would have killed himself, supposing that the prisoners had been fled.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There was an earthquake, that the keeper should be roused from sleep, and the doors flew open, that he should wonder at what had happened: but these things the prisoners saw not: otherwise they would all have fled: but the keeper of the prison was about to slay himself, thinking the prisoners were escaped.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Paul cried with a loud voice, saying, Do thyself no harm: for we are all here” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 28.) (b) “Then he called for lights, and sprang in, and came trembling, and fell down before Paul and Silas; and brought them out, and said, Sirs, what must I do to be saved?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark how the wonder overpowered him? (a) He wondered more at Paul’s kindness; he was amazed at his manly boldness, that he had not escaped when he had it in his power, that he hindered him from killing himself. (c) “And they said, believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved, and thy house.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they spake unto him the word of the Lord, and to all that were in his house.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 31, 35) and (so) immediately gave proof of their kindness towards him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he took them the same hour of the night and washed their stripes; and was baptized, he and all his, straightway.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He washed them, and was himself baptized, he and his house.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he had brought them into his house, he set meat before them, and rejoiced, believing in God with all his house.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when it was day, the magistrates sent the sergeants, saying, Let those men go.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is likely the magistrates had learnt what had happened, and did not dare of themselves to dismiss them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the keeper of the prison told these words to Paul, saying, the magistrates have sent to let you go: now therefore depart, and go in peace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Paul said unto them, they have beaten us openly uncondemned, being Romans, and have cast us into prison; and now do they thrust as out privily? nay verily; but let them come themselves and fetch us out.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the sergeants told these words unto the magistrates: and they feared, when they heard that they were Romans.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they came and besought them, and brought them out, and desired them to depart out of the city.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they went out of the prison, and entered into the house of Lydia: and when they had seen the brethren, they comforted them, and departed.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Even upon the declaration of the magistrates Paul does not go out, but for the sake both of Lydia and the rest he puts them in fear: that they may not be supposed to have come out upon their own request, that they may set the rest in a posture of boldness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The impeachment was twofold: that “being Romans,” and “uncondemned,” they had openly cast them into prison.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You see that in many things they took their measures as men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation) “And at midnight,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us compare, beloved, with that night these nights of ours, with their revellings, their drunkenness, and wanton excesses, with their sleep which might as well be death, their watchings which are worse than sleep.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For while some sleep without sense or feeling, others lie awake to pitiable and wretched purpose, plotting deceits, anxiously thinking about money, studying how they may be revenged upon those who do them wrong, meditating enmity, reckoning up the abusive words spoken during the day: thus do they rake up the smouldering embers of wrath, doing things intolerable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how Peter slept. (ch. xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both there, it was wisely ordered (that he should be asleep); for the Angel came to him, and it behooved that none should see what happened; and on the other hand it was well ordered here (that Paul should be awake), in order that the keeper of the prison might be prevented from killing himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And suddenly there was a great earthquake.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why did no other miracle take place?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because this was, of all others, the thing sufficient for his conversion, seeing he was personally in danger: for it is not so much miracles that overpower us, as the things which issue in our own deliverance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That the earthquake should not seem to have come of itself, there was this concurrent circumstance, bearing witness to it: “the doors were opened, and all their bonds were loosed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it appears in the night-time; for the Apostles did not work for display, but for men’s salvation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the keeper of the prison,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The keeper was not an evil-disposed man that he “thrust them into the inner prison,” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24) was because of his “having received such a command,” not of himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The man was all in a tumult of perturbation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What shall I do to be saved?” he asks.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why not before this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Paul shouted, until he saw, and is beforehand with him saying, “We are all here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And having called for lights,” it says, “he sprang in, and fell down at the feet” of the prisoner; he, the prison keeper, saying, “Sirs, what must I do to be saved?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, what had they said?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, he does not, on finding himself safe, think all is well; he is overcome with awe at the miraculous power.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark what happened in the former case, and what here?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There a girl was released from a spirit, and they cast them into prison, because they had liberated her from the spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here, they did but show the doors standing open, and it opened the doors of his heart, it loosed two sorts of chains; that (prisoner) kindled the (true) light; for the light in his heart was shining.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he sprang in, and fell before them;” and he does not ask, How is this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is this? but straightway he says, “What must I do to be saved?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then answers Paul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved, thou and thine house.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this above all, wins men: that one’s house also should be saved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they spake the word to him, and to all that were in his house.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he took them the same hour of the night, and washed their stripes,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 32, 33), washed them and was washed: those he washed from their stripes, himself was washed from his sins: he fed and was fed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And rejoiced,” it says: although there was nothing but words only and good hopes: “having believed in God with all his house (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 34): this was the token of his having believed—that he was released of all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What worse than a jailer, what more ruthless, more savage?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He entertained them with great honor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not, because he was safe, he made merry, but, having believed God. (a) “Believe on the Lord,” said the Apostle: therefore it is that the writer here says, “Having believed.—(d) Now therefore,” it says, “depart, and go in peace” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 36): that is, in safety, fearing no man. (b) “But Paul said unto them” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 37): that he may not seem to be receiving his liberty as one condemned, and as one that has done wrong: therefore it is that he says, “Having openly beaten us uncondemned,” etc.—that it may not be matter of grace on their part. (e) And besides, they wish the jailer himself to be out of danger, that he may not be called to account for this afterwards.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they do not say, “Having beaten us,” who have wrought miracles: for they (the magistrates) did not even heed these: but, that which was most effectual to shake their minds, “uncondemned, and being Romans.” (c) Observe how diversely grace manages things: how Peter went out, how Paul, though both were Apostles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “They feared,” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 38) it says: because the men were Romans, not because they had unjustly cast them into prison, “And besought them to depart out of the city” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 39): begged them as a favor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they went to the house of Lydia, and having confirmed her, so departed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it was not right to leave their hostess in distress and anxiety.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they went out, not in compliance with the request of those rulers, but hasting to the preaching: the city having been sufficiently benefited by the miracle: for it was fit they should not be there any longer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in the absence of them that wrought it, the miracle appeared greater, itself crying out more loudly: the faith of the jailer was a voice in itself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What equal to this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is put in bonds, and looses, being bound: looses a twofold bond: him that bound him, he looses by being bound.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These are indeed works of (supernatural) grace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (f) Let us constantly bear in mind this jailer, not the miracle: how, prisoner as he was (the Apostle), persuaded his jailer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What say the heathen?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And of what things,” say they, “was such a man as this to be persuaded—a vile, wretched creature, of no understanding, full of all that is bad and nothing else, and easily brought over to anything?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For these, say they, are the things, a tanner, a purple-seller, an eunuch, slaves, and women believed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is what they say.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then will they be able to say, when we produce the men of rank and station, the centurion, the proconsul, those from that time to the present, the rulers themselves, the emperors?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But for my part, I speak of something else, greater than this: let us look to these very persons of no consideration.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And where is the wonder?” say you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, this, I say, is a wonder.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, if a person be persuaded about any common things, it is no wonder: but if resurrection, a kingdom of heaven, a life of philosophic self-command, be the subjects, and, discoursing of these to persons of mean consideration, one persuades them, it will be more wonderful than if one persuaded wise men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when there is no danger attending the things of which one persuades people, then (the objector) might with some plausibility allege want of sense on their part: but when (the preacher) says—to the slave, as you will have it—“If thou be persuaded by me, it is at thy peril, thou wilt have all men for thine enemies, thou must die, thou must suffer evils without number,” and yet for all this, convinces that man’s soul, there can be no more talk here of want of sense.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since, if indeed the doctrines contained what was pleasant, one might fairly enough say this: but if, what the philosohers would never have chosen to learn, this the slave does learn, then is the wonder greater.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, if you will, let us bring before us the tanner himself, and see what were the subjects on which Peter conversed with him: or if you will, this same jailer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then said Paul to him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “That Christ rose again,” say you; “that there is a resurrection of the dead, and a kingdom: and he had no difficulty in persuading him, a man easily led to anything.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Said he nothing about the mode of life; that he must be temperate, that he must be superior to money, that he must not be unmerciful, that he must impart of his good things to others?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it cannot be said, that the being persuaded to these things also was from the want of power of mind; no, to be brought to all this required a great soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For be it so, that as far as the doctrines went, they were rendered more apt to receive these by their want of intelligence: but to accept such a virtuous, self-denying rule of life, how could that be owing to any defect of understanding?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that the less understanding the person may have, if nevertheless he is persuaded to things, to which even philosophers were unable to persuade their fellow-philosophers, the greater the wonder—when women and slaves are persuaded of these truths, and prove it by their actions, of which same truths the Platos and all the rest of them were never able to persuade any man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why say I, “any man?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say rather, not themselves even: on the contrary, that money is not to be despised, Plato persuaded (his disciples) by getting, as he did, such an abundance of property, and golden rings, and goblets; and that the honor to be had from the many is not to be despised, this Socrates himself shows, for all that he may philosophize without end on this point: for in everything he did, he had an eye to fame.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And if you were conversant with his discourses, I might go at great length into this subject, and show what a deal of insincerity (εἰρωνείαν) there was in them,—if at least we may believe what his disciple says of him,—and how that all his writings have their ground-work in vainglory.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, leaving them, let us direct the discourse to our own selves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For besides the things that have been said, there is this also to be added, that men were persuaded of these things to their own peril.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be not thou therefore shameless, but let us think over that night, the stocks, and the hymns of praise.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This let us also do, and we shall open for ourselves—not a prison, but—heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we pray, we shall be able even to open heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Elias both shut and opened heaven by prayer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is a prison in heaven also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whatsoever,” He saith, “ye shall bind on earth, shall be bound in heaven.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us pray by night, and we shall loose these bonds.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that prayers loose sins, let that widow convince us, let that friend convince us, who at that untimely hour of the night persists and knocks (Luke xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 5): let Cornelius convince us, for, “thy prayers,” it says, “and thine alms are come up before God.” (ch. x.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let Paul convince us, who says, “Now she that is a widow indeed and desolate, trusteth in God, and continueth in supplications night and day.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If he speaks thus of a widow, a weak woman, much more would he of men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I have both before discoursed to you on this, and now repeat it: let us arouse ourselves during the night: though thou make not many prayers, make one with watchfulness, and it is enough, I ask no more: and if not at midnight, at any rate at the first dawn.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Show that the night is not only for the body, but also for the soul: do not suffer it to pass idly, but make this return to thy Master: nay rather (the benefit) itself returns to thee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, if we fall into any difficult strait, to whom do we not make request? and if we soon obtain our request, we breathe freely again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What a boon were it for thee, to have a friend to go to with thy request, who shall be ready to take it as a kindness, and to be obliged to thee for thy asking?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What a boon, not to have to go about and seek one to ask of, but to find one ready? to have no need of others through whom thou mayest solicit?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What could be greater than this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since here is One who then does most, when we make not our requests of others than Himself: just as a sincere friend then most complains of us for not trusting in his friendship, when we ask of others to make request to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus also let us act.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But what,” you will ask, “if I should have offended Him?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Cease to give offence, and weep, and so draw near to Him, and thou wilt quickly render Him propitious as to thy former sins.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say only, I have offended: say it from thy soul and with a sincere mind, and all things are remitted to thee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou dost not so much desire thy sins to be forgiven, as He desires to forgive thee thy sins.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In proof that thou dost not so desire it, consider that thou hast no mind either to practice vigils, or to give thy money freely: but He, that He might forgive our sins, spared not His Only-begotten and True Son, the partner of His throne.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou how He more desires to forgive thee thy sins (than thou to be forgiven )?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us not be slothful, nor put off this any longer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is merciful and good: only let us give Him an opportunity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And (even) this (He seeks), only that we may not become unprofitable, since even without this He could have freed us from them: but like as we (with the same view) devise and arrange many things for our servants to do, so does He in the matter of our salvation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Let us anticipate His face with thanksgiving.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Let us come before His presence.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: E.V.), since He is good and kind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if thou call not upon Him, what will He do?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou dost not choose to say, Forgive; thou wilt not say it from thy heart, but with thy mouth only.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is it, to call in truth?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (To call) with purpose of heart, with earnestness, with a sincere mind; just as men say of perfumes, “This is genuine, and has nothing spurious,” so here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He who truly calls on Him, he who truly prays to Him, continually attends to it, and desists not, until he obtain (his request): but he who does it in a merely formal manner (ἀφοσιούμενος), and even this only by way of fulfilling a law, does not call in truth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whosoever thou art, say not only, “I am a sinner,” but be earnest also to rid thyself of this character; say not this only, but also grieve.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou grievest, thou art in earnest: if thou art not in earnest, thou grievest not: if thou grievest not, thou triflest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What sort of man is he who shall say, “I am sick,” and not to do all to be freed from his sickness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A mighty weapon is Prayer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “If ye,” saith the Lord, “know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more your Father?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then wherefore art thou unwilling to approach Him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He loves thee, He is of more power than all besides.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both willing is He and able, what is there to hinder?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But then, on our part, let us draw near with faith, draw near, offering the gifts that He desires, forgetfulness of wrongs, kindness, meekness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Though thou be a sinner, with boldness shalt thou ask of Him forgiveness of thy sins, if thou canst show that this has been done by thyself: but though thou be righteous, and possess not this virtue of forgetfulness of injuries, thou art none the better for it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It cannot be that a man who has forgiven his neighbor should not obtain perfect forgiveness: for God is beyond comparison more merciful than we.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What sayest thou?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou sayest, “I have been wronged, I have subdued my anger, I have endured the onset of wrath because of Thy command, and dost Thou not forgive?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Full surely He will forgive: and this is plain to all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore let us purge our soul from all resentment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is sufficient for us, in order that we may be heard; and let us pray with watching and much perseverance, that having enjoyed His bountiful mercy, we may be found worthy of the good things promised, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father, together with the Holy Spirit, be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
